docs.text: Qortex reads sidecars, event tables, participants, and root metadata before it downloads large imaging files. This is the cheapest way to learn whether a dataset has labels, usable demographics, task timing, TR, channel metadata, or companion files.

docs.title: Real Participants Example
docs.text:
This figure is generated by scripts/generate_docs_examples.py from public OpenNeuro dataset ds000001:

from qortex import Dataset

ds = Dataset("ds000001")
participants = ds.participants(prefer_api=False)
print(participants.shape)
print(participants.head())

Observed shape:

(16, 3)
Real `participants.tsv` data from `ds000001`: 16 participants, ages 19-31, and sex counts read through `Dataset.participants(prefer_api=False)`.

docs.title: Dataset-Level Metadata
docs.text:
from qortex import Dataset

ds = Dataset("ds000001")

metadata_files = ds.metadata_files()
participants = ds.participants()
description = ds.preview("dataset_description.json")

participants() returns a Polars dataframe. Qortex tries OpenNeuro API summary metadata first; pass prefer_api=False to read participants.tsv directly from the CDN.

docs.title: File-Level Sidecars
docs.text:
sidecar = ds.sidecar(
    "sub-01/func/sub-01_task-balloonanalogrisktask_run-01_bold.nii.gz"
)
events = ds.events(subject="01", task="balloonanalogrisktask", run="01")

print(sidecar["RepetitionTime"])
print(events.select(["onset", "duration", "trial_type"]).head())

sidecar() accepts a BIDS-relative path or file record. It merges JSON sidecars through BIDS inheritance where possible. events() fetches the matching events.tsv; pass subject, session, task, and run to disambiguate.

docs.title: CLI
docs.text:
List metadata and sidecar/table files:

qortex metadata ds000001 --limit 20

Download metadata only:

qortex metadata ds000001 --download --output-dir data/ds000001-meta

Preview a specific remote file:

qortex preview ds000001 participants.tsv --rows 5
qortex preview ds000001 sub-01/func/sub-01_task-balloonanalogrisktask_run-01_events.tsv --rows 5

docs.title: What Metadata Can Decide
docs.text:
Metadata What it answers dataset_description.json Dataset name, BIDS version, license, generated-by metadata. participants.tsv Subject IDs, age/sex/group columns when provided. *_events.tsv Candidate label columns, onsets, durations, class counts, trial timing. JSON sidecars TR, task name, sampling frequency, units, slice timing, acquisition metadata. channels.tsv Channel names, types, units, and bad-channel flags. bval / bvec DWI shell structure and gradient companions.

This information is often enough to decide whether the full dataset is worth downloading.

docs.title: Evidence
docs.text:
Real `participants.tsv` loaded through `Dataset.participants(prefer_api=False)`. 
participants = ds.participants(prefer_api=False)
print(participants.shape)

Result artifact: ds000001-example-results.json

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Inspect
  • Label readiness
  • Can train
  • Metadata-only download

docs.title: Search & Filter
docs.text: Qortex offers two search paths: a local DuckDB catalog (fast, offline, incremental) and live search against the OpenNeuro GraphQL API (always current, no prior refresh needed). Both expose the same fluent DatasetQuery interface.

docs.title: Quick reference
docs.text: Need Method Fast filtered search (offline) DatasetQuery().modality("eeg").fetch() Always-current search DatasetQuery().modality("eeg").live() Rich metadata + engagement sort client.search_datasets_rich(modality="eeg", sort_by="downloads") Free-text across name/description DatasetQuery().containing("auditory cortex").fetch() Single dataset — all metadata client.get_dataset_rich("ds008039")

docs.title: DatasetQuery — fluent builder
docs.text:
DatasetQuery chains filter methods and terminates with .fetch() (local catalog) or .live() (API).

from qortex.catalog import DatasetQuery

# Local catalog — fast, works offline
results = (
    DatasetQuery()
    .modality("eeg")
    .task("rest")
    .min_subjects(30)
    .containing("auditory")
    .has_events()
    .limit(20)
    .fetch()
)

for row in results:
    print(row["dataset_id"], row["n_subjects"], row["size_gb"])

docs.title: Available filters
docs.text: Method Type Description .containing(text) str Free-text match on dataset name and description .modality(mod) str Filter by modality: "eeg", "mri", "fmri", "meg", "ieeg", "pet", "nirs" .task(task) str Filter by task name (substring match) .author(name) str Filter by author name (substring match) .license(name) str Filter by license: "CC0", "CC-BY", "PDDL", etc. .min_subjects(n) int Minimum number of subjects .max_size_gb(gb) float Maximum total dataset size in GB .has_events(True) bool Only datasets with events.tsv files .has_derivatives(True) bool Only datasets with a derivatives/ directory .limit(n) int Maximum results (default: 50) .offset(n) int Pagination offset

docs.title: Execution methods
docs.text:
# Local catalog
results = query.fetch()             # list[dict]
page    = query.fetch_page()        # PageResult(results, total, offset)

# Live API (always current, no catalog refresh needed)
results = query.live()              # list[RichDatasetInfo]
results = query.live(sync_local=True)  # also updates local catalog

# Facet counts (distribution of modalities, tasks, licenses)
facets = DatasetQuery().modality("eeg").facets()
print(facets["tasks"])    # {"rest": 42, "nback": 18, ...}

docs.title: Refresh the local catalog
docs.text:
Pull the latest index from OpenNeuro once and search it offline:

qortex catalog-refresh

Writes to ~/.qortex/catalog.duckdb. Incremental on subsequent runs.

docs.title: CLI search
docs.text:
qortex search --modality eeg
qortex search --modality eeg --task rest
qortex search --modality eeg --min-subjects 50
qortex search --modality fmri --task nback --min-subjects 30
qortex search --max-size 10           # GB
qortex search --query "resting state" # free-text on name/description
qortex search --has-events
qortex search --license CC0

Results:

ID         Name                          Subjects  Size    Modalities
ds004130   EEG resting-state alpha...    88        4.2 GB  eeg
ds003490   Resting state EEG...          64        2.1 GB  eeg

Add --json or --csv for machine-readable output.

docs.title: Rich search via the API
docs.text:
search_datasets_rich() searches the OpenNeuro API directly and returns fully populated RichDatasetInfo objects — with engagement metrics, demographics, funding, and snapshot summaries.

from qortex.client import OpenNeuroClient

with OpenNeuroClient() as client:
    results = client.search_datasets_rich(
        modality="eeg",
        task="rest",
        min_subjects=30,
        min_downloads=100,
        sort_by="downloads",  # "downloads" | "views" | "stars" | "subjects" | "size" | "recent"
        limit=20,
    )

for info in results:
    snap = info.latest_snapshot_summary
    print(
        info.id,
        info.name,
        snap.n_subjects,
        info.engagement.downloads,
        info.engagement.popularity_score,
    )

docs.title: Sort options for search_datasets_rich
docs.text: sort_by Orders by "downloads" Most downloaded first (default) "views" Most viewed first "stars" Most starred first "subjects" Most subjects first "size" Largest datasets first "recent" Most recently published first

docs.title: Combining search with inspect
docs.text:
A typical workflow: search for candidates, then inspect the most promising ones:

from qortex.catalog import DatasetQuery
from qortex.client import OpenNeuroClient

# Step 1 — find candidates (local, fast)
candidates = (
    DatasetQuery()
    .modality("eeg")
    .min_subjects(50)
    .has_events()
    .limit(5)
    .fetch()
)

# Step 2 — get full metadata for each
with OpenNeuroClient() as client:
    for row in candidates:
        info = client.get_dataset_rich(row["dataset_id"])
        readme = client.get_readme(row["dataset_id"])
        print(info.id, info.engagement.downloads, info.grant_funder)
        print(readme[:200] if readme else "(no README)")

docs.title: Catalog profile
docs.text:
qortex catalog-profile

Shows: total datasets indexed, last refresh timestamp, modality distribution.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Inspect — full metadata and file-level detail for a specific dataset
  • Snapshots — list available versions of a dataset
  • Metadata — read sidecar files without downloading imaging data

docs.title: Snapshots
docs.text: A snapshot is a versioned, immutable copy of an OpenNeuro dataset. Every time a dataset depositor updates their data, a new snapshot is created with an incremented version number. Old snapshots remain accessible indefinitely.

docs.title: Why snapshots matter for reproducibility
docs.text:
If you pin a snapshot version, the manifest and all CDN file URLs remain constant. The same Qortex command will produce the same download regardless of when it runs.

If you do not pin a snapshot, Qortex uses the latest published snapshot at the time you first fetch the manifest. Subsequent runs may resolve to a different (newer) snapshot.

docs.title: List available snapshots
docs.text:
from qortex.client import OpenNeuroClient

with OpenNeuroClient() as client:
    snapshots = client.get_snapshots("ds004130")

for s in snapshots:
    print(s.tag, s.created, s.size)

From the CLI:

qortex metadata ds004130 --snapshots

Output:

ds004130 snapshots:
  1.0.0   2022-03-15   87 subjects   4.1 GB
  1.1.0   2022-08-20   88 subjects   4.2 GB
  1.2.0   2023-01-10   88 subjects   4.2 GB  ← latest

docs.title: Pin a snapshot
docs.text:
from qortex import Dataset

ds = Dataset("ds004130", snapshot="1.0.0")
profile = ds.inspect()
print(profile.n_subjects)  # 87

From the CLI, add --snapshot:

qortex inspect ds004130 --snapshot 1.0.0
qortex download ds004130 --snapshot 1.0.0 --output-dir data/ds004130_v1/

docs.title: Get summary for a specific snapshot
docs.text:
from qortex.client import OpenNeuroClient

with OpenNeuroClient() as client:
    summary = client.get_snapshot_summary("ds004130", "1.0.0")

print(summary.n_subjects)    # 87
print(summary.total_size_gb) # 4.1
print(summary.bids_version)  # "1.6.0"
print(summary.tasks)         # ["rest"]
print(summary.sessions)      # []
print(summary.funding)       # ["NIH ..."]

docs.title: Compare snapshots
docs.text:
To see which files changed between two snapshots:

from qortex import Dataset

ds_old = Dataset("ds004130", snapshot="1.0.0")
ds_new = Dataset("ds004130", snapshot="1.2.0")

old_paths = {f.path for f in ds_old.manifest().files}
new_paths = {f.path for f in ds_new.manifest().files}

added   = new_paths - old_paths
removed = old_paths - new_paths
print(f"Added: {len(added)}  Removed: {len(removed)}")

docs.title: Recording the snapshot in provenance
docs.text:
When you convert a dataset, the snapshot version is recorded in the artifact manifest automatically:

art = ds.convert(...)
print(art.manifest.source_snapshot)  # "ds004130@1.2.0"

This lets you trace any artifact back to the exact dataset version it came from.

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• Very old snapshots (pre-2020) may have stale CDN URLs that return 404. Use a newer snapshot if possible.
  • The snapshot list is fetched from the OpenNeuro GraphQL API. A network error here means you cannot list snapshots.

docs.title: Evidence
docs.text:
Real `Dataset.manifest()` output from OpenNeuro ds000001: suffix counts and bytes by BIDS datatype. 
ds = Dataset('ds000001', snapshot='1.0.0')
manifest = ds.manifest()

Result artifact: ds000001-example-results.json

docs.title: Download
docs.text: Qortex downloads files from the OpenNeuro CDN to a local directory. The download engine supports resume, companion file inclusion, and selective filtering.

docs.title: Core principle: plan before you download
docs.text:
Qortex separates planning from execution. ds.plan() returns a DownloadPlan that lists exact files with target paths. You can inspect the plan before committing to the transfer.

plan = ds.plan(subjects=["01", "02"], tasks=["rest"])
print(f"{len(plan.files)} files, {plan.size_gb:.1f} GB")
ds.download_paths(plan.files)

Or combine planning and execution:

ds.download(subjects=["01", "02"], tasks=["rest"], data_dir="data/ds004130/")

docs.title: What you can do
docs.text:
Plan — Create a DownloadPlan and inspect it before downloading.

Selective download — Filter by subject, session, task, run, suffix, and size.

Metadata only — Download only sidecar JSON, events.tsv, bval/bvec, and participants files.

Cache — Where files are stored, how the cache works, and how to clear it.

Local index — Scan a pre-existing BIDS directory and build a local manifest.

Resume — How interrupted downloads recover.

Next → Visualize — QC your local files before conversion to catch bad data early.

docs.title: Evidence
docs.text:
Real `minimum(goal='first-batch')` plan: metadata, sidecar, events, and one BOLD run. 
plan = ds.minimum(goal='first-batch', output_dir=Path('data/ds000001'))
print(plan.to_text())

Result artifact: ds000001-minimum-first-batch.txt

docs.title: Cache
docs.text: Qortex stores downloaded files in a local cache directory. Understanding how the cache works prevents duplicate downloads and helps manage disk space.

docs.title: Default cache location
docs.text:
~/.qortex/data/{dataset_id}/{snapshot}/

For example:

~/.qortex/data/ds004130/1.2.0/sub-01/eeg/sub-01_task-rest_eeg.set

Override the default with data_dir:

ds.download(data_dir="/scratch/datasets/ds004130/")

When data_dir is set, files go directly into that directory (without the ~/.qortex/data/ prefix).

docs.title: Cache inspection from CLI
docs.text:
# Show total cache size and dataset list
qortex cache info

# List cached datasets with sizes
qortex cache list

# Show what is cached for a specific dataset
qortex cache list ds004130

Output example:

ds004130
  Snapshot: 1.2.0
  Path:     ~/.qortex/data/ds004130/1.2.0/
  Files:    1,056
  Size:     4.2 GB
  Last used: 2024-01-15

docs.title: Remove cache entries
docs.text:
# Remove all cached files for a dataset
qortex cache remove ds004130

# Remove a specific snapshot
qortex cache remove ds004130 --snapshot 1.0.0

# Clear the entire cache (all datasets)
qortex cache clear

From Python:

from qortex.cache import clear_cache, remove_dataset_cache

clear_cache()
remove_dataset_cache("ds004130", snapshot="1.0.0")

docs.title: Avoiding duplicate downloads
docs.text:
If a file already exists at its target path and its size matches the manifest record, Qortex skips the download. Qortex does not compare checksums during the skip check — it only checks file size.

If you suspect a file is corrupted, delete it manually and re-download.

docs.title: Disk space planning
docs.text:
Before downloading, estimate required space:

plan = ds.plan(subjects=["01", "02", "03"], tasks=["rest"])
print(f"Required: {plan.size_gb:.1f} GB")

docs.title: Manifests and sidecar caches
docs.text:
The manifest JSON and fetched sidecar files are cached at:

~/.qortex/manifests/{dataset_id}/{snapshot}.json
~/.qortex/sidecars/{dataset_id}/{snapshot}/{path}.json

These are small (< 10 MB per dataset) and are kept indefinitely. Delete them if you need to force a manifest re-fetch:

rm ~/.qortex/manifests/ds004130/1.2.0.json

Or force refresh in Python:

manifest = ds.manifest(force_refresh=True)

docs.title: Evidence
docs.text:
Local availability check for the `ds000001` first-batch plan on this machine. 
qortex content-status data/ds000001 --dataset ds000001

Result artifact: neuroai-fixture-summary.json

docs.title: Local Index
docs.text:
If you already have a BIDS dataset on disk — from OpenNeuro CLI, DataLad, or your institution's storage — you can point Qortex at it without downloading anything.

index_local() scans the local directory and builds a manifest that Qortex uses for all subsequent operations.

docs.title: Python
docs.text:
from qortex import Dataset

ds = Dataset("ds004130", data_dir="/scratch/datasets/ds004130/")
ds.index_local()

# Now all readiness and conversion commands work
report = ds.doctor()
art = ds.convert(output_dir="artifacts/")

docs.title: CLI
docs.text:
qortex local-index /scratch/datasets/ds004130/ --dataset-id ds004130

After indexing, pass --local to other commands to use the local manifest:

qortex inspect ds004130 --local
qortex doctor ds004130 --local

docs.title: What local indexing does
docs.text:
1. Walks the directory tree recursively
  2. Parses BIDS entity labels from each file path
  3. Computes file sizes from disk
  4. Resolves companion relationships
  5. Writes .qortex/manifest.json inside the data directory

The local manifest does not include CDN URLs. If you want to download missing files after indexing, Qortex fetches the remote manifest and cross-references with the local index.

docs.title: Checking for missing files after indexing
docs.text:
ds.index_local()
report = ds.doctor()
# report.missing_files lists files in the remote manifest that are absent locally

Or use visual audit:

report = ds.visual_audit(local_path="/scratch/datasets/ds004130/", output_dir="qc/ds004130")
print(report.summary())

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• Local indexing does not validate BIDS structure. It only parses entity labels from path names. Run ds.validate() for structural validation.
  • Files that do not follow BIDS naming conventions are indexed but have None for most entity fields. They appear in the manifest with unknown datatype and suffix.
  • Symlinks are followed during indexing. If the link target does not exist, the file is skipped.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Cache — where Qortex stores downloaded files
  • Validate — check the local BIDS structure after indexing

docs.title: Metadata-Only Download
docs.text: A metadata-only download fetches everything except the large imaging files. The result is a local directory with JSON sidecars, TSV files, bval/bvec files, and root-level metadata — but no NIfTIs, EEG recordings, or DICOM files.

docs.title: Why metadata first
docs.text:
Before spending an hour downloading gigabytes of NIfTI data, a metadata-only pass answers:

  • Are events.tsv files present for all subjects? (label readiness)
  • What trial types are in the events files? (class distribution)
  • What is the TR, number of volumes, and slice order? (preprocessing planning)
  • How many EEG channels and what type? (channel configuration)
  • What b-values appear in the DWI acquisition? (shell count)

This information costs seconds to fetch and requires almost no disk space.

docs.title: Python
docs.text:
from qortex import Dataset

ds = Dataset("ds004130")
ds.download_metadata(data_dir="data/ds004130/")

Files downloaded:

  • dataset_description.json
  • participants.tsv
  • README
  • CHANGES
  • *_bold.json, *_T1w.json, *_eeg.json, etc. (all sidecar JSON)
  • *_events.tsv, *_channels.tsv, *_electrodes.tsv, *_coordsystem.json
  • *.bval, *.bvec
  • *_physio.json (not the .tsv.gz)

Imaging files excluded:

  • *.nii.gz, *.nii
  • *.set, *.edf, *.fif, *.cnt
  • *.dcm and DICOM directories
  • *.tgz, *.zip

docs.title: CLI
docs.text: qortex metadata ds004130 --download --output-dir data/ds004130/

docs.title: Check label coverage after metadata download
docs.text:
After a metadata download, the events.tsv files are present. Use ds.label_landscape() to see the label distribution across subjects:

ds = Dataset("ds004130", data_dir="data/ds004130/")
print(ds.label_landscape())

Output:

trial_type  count  subjects_with_label
rest         88    88/88
eyes-open    86    86/88  ← 2 subjects missing
task         88    88/88

docs.title: Size estimate
docs.text: For most datasets, a metadata-only download is 1–5 MB total, regardless of dataset size. The events.tsv files are the largest component.

docs.title: Evidence
docs.text:
Real `minimum(goal='first-batch')` plan: metadata, sidecar, events, and one BOLD run. 
plan = ds.minimum(goal='first-batch', output_dir=Path('data/ds000001'))
print(plan.to_text())

Result artifact: ds000001-minimum-first-batch.txt

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Selective download — download specific imaging files after metadata review
  • Label readiness — check label coverage after metadata download

docs.title: Plan
docs.text: ds.plan() constructs a DownloadPlan without starting any transfers. The plan lists every file that would be downloaded, its target local path, and the CDN URL.

docs.title: Basic usage
docs.text:
from qortex import Dataset

ds = Dataset("ds004130")
plan = ds.plan(subjects=["01", "02"], tasks=["rest"], suffixes=["eeg"])

print(f"Files: {len(plan.files)}")
print(f"Size:  {plan.size_gb:.2f} GB")
print(f"Target: {plan.target_dir}")

for f in plan.files[:5]:
    print(f.path, f.size)

docs.title: Plan properties
docs.text: Property Type Description files list[FileRecord] Files that will be downloaded target_dir Path Root directory for downloads size_gb float Total size in GB n_subjects int Number of subjects in plan includes_companions bool Whether companions were added

docs.title: Serialize the plan
docs.text:
import json

plan_dict = plan.to_dict()
with open("plan.json", "w") as f:
    json.dump(plan_dict, f, indent=2)

Reload and execute later:

from qortex.download import DownloadPlan

plan = DownloadPlan.from_file("plan.json")
ds.download_paths(plan.files)

docs.title: CLI
docs.text:
qortex plan ds004130 --subjects 01 02 --tasks rest --suffixes eeg

Output shows file count and total size. Add --output plan.json to save:

qortex plan ds004130 --subjects 01 02 --output plan.json

docs.title: Minimum plan
docs.text:
ds.minimum() builds a plan for the smallest set of subjects that enables a specific goal:

plan = ds.minimum(goal="first-batch")
plan = ds.minimum(goal="label-check")
plan = ds.minimum(goal="validation")
plan = ds.minimum(goal="metadata")

first-batch picks enough subjects (usually 3–5) to run one full pipeline pass: download → convert → train step.

docs.title: Companion file inclusion
docs.text:
By default, any imaging file in the plan brings its companions with it. A BOLD NIfTI automatically includes its JSON sidecar and events.tsv. To suppress this:

plan = ds.plan(subjects=["01"], include_companions=False)

Excluding companions is rarely useful — conversion will fail without sidecars and events.

docs.title: Evidence
docs.text:
Real `minimum(goal='first-batch')` plan: metadata, sidecar, events, and one BOLD run. 
plan = ds.minimum(goal='first-batch', output_dir=Path('data/ds000001'))
print(plan.to_text())

Result artifact: ds000001-minimum-first-batch.txt

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Selective download — filter options
  • Resume — what happens if a transfer is interrupted mid-plan

docs.title: Resume
docs.text: Downloads interrupted by network errors, timeouts, or Ctrl-C resume automatically. Qortex uses HTTP range requests to continue from where a transfer stopped.

docs.title: How it works
docs.text:
Qortex tracks download progress per file using a .part file alongside the destination path. When a download resumes:

  1. The .part file is detected at the target location
  2. Qortex issues a Range: bytes={size}- HTTP request for the remaining bytes
  3. The partial content is appended to the .part file
  4. On completion, the .part file is atomically moved to the final path

If the CDN does not support range requests (rare), Qortex falls back to restarting the file from the beginning.

docs.title: Resuming a stopped download
docs.text:
Simply re-run the same download command:

ds.download(subjects=["01", "02"], data_dir="data/ds004130/")
# stopped at 60% of sub-02 EEG file

# Run again — picks up from where it stopped
ds.download(subjects=["01", "02"], data_dir="data/ds004130/")

Files already fully downloaded are skipped. The partial file resumes.

docs.title: Parallel downloads
docs.text:
Qortex uses a thread pool for concurrent file transfers. The default concurrency is 4 simultaneous downloads. Override with:

ds.download(subjects=["01"], concurrency=8, data_dir="data/")

Or set in configuration:

qortex download ds004130 --output-dir data/ds004130

Higher concurrency does not always improve throughput — CDN rate limiting may kick in above 4–8 concurrent streams.

docs.title: Handling CDN errors
docs.text:
OpenNeuro CDN links expire after a few hours. If a download fails with HTTP 403 or 410, the link has expired. Re-running ds.download() fetches fresh links before retrying.

Qortex automatically refreshes expired CDN links during a resume — you do not need to restart from scratch.

docs.title: Integrity check after download
docs.text:
After all files complete, Qortex checks that each file's size on disk matches the expected size from the manifest. Files with size mismatches are logged as warnings:

WARNING: sub-03_task-rest_bold.nii.gz size mismatch: expected 450 MB, got 312 MB

To re-download size-mismatched files:

ds.download(subjects=["03"], force=True, data_dir="data/")

force=True re-downloads even files that already exist at the correct size.

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• Checksums are not verified after download. Qortex only checks file size. A file with the right size but corrupted content will pass.
  • If the manifest changes between the initial download and a resume (e.g., a snapshot was updated), some CDN URLs may differ. Pin a snapshot to avoid this.

docs.title: Selective Download
docs.text: You rarely need an entire OpenNeuro dataset. Selective download lets you specify exactly which subjects, sessions, tasks, runs, and file types to transfer.

docs.title: Filter parameters
docs.text:
All filter parameters are accepted by both ds.download() and ds.plan():

Parameter Type Example subjects list[str] ["01", "02", "03"] sessions list[str] ["baseline", "followup"] tasks list[str] ["rest", "nback"] runs list[str] ["01", "02"] suffixes list[str] ["bold", "T1w"] datatypes list[str] ["func", "anat"] include list[str] glob patterns to include exclude list[str] glob patterns to exclude max_size_gb float skip files larger than this

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Download resting-state BOLD from the first 10 subjects:

ds.download(
    subjects=[f"{i:02d}" for i in range(1, 11)],
    tasks=["rest"],
    suffixes=["bold"],
    data_dir="data/ds004130/",
)

Download all EEG files including events:

ds.download(
    datatypes=["eeg"],
    data_dir="data/ds004130/",
)

Download using glob patterns:

ds.download(
    include=["sub-01/**", "sub-02/**"],
    exclude=["**/*_desc-confounds_*"],
    data_dir="data/ds004130/",
)

Skip files larger than 500 MB:

ds.download(max_size_gb=0.5, data_dir="data/ds004130/")

docs.title: CLI
docs.text:
# Subjects 01–10, resting-state BOLD
qortex download ds004130 \
    --subjects 01 02 03 04 05 06 07 08 09 10 \
    --tasks rest \
    --suffixes bold \
    --output-dir data/ds004130/

# All EEG
qortex download ds004130 --modalities eeg --output-dir data/ds004130/

# Skip large files
qortex download ds004130 --modalities eeg --output-dir data/ds004130/ --dry-run

docs.title: Companion files
docs.text:
When you download a primary imaging file, its companions (sidecar JSON, events.tsv, bval/bvec) are included automatically. The companion inclusion follows the BIDS specification:

  • BOLD → includes *_bold.json, *_events.tsv, optionally *_desc-confounds_timeseries.tsv
  • DWI → includes *.bval, *.bvec, *_dwi.json
  • EEG → includes *_eeg.json, *_channels.tsv, *_coordsystem.json

Root-level essential files (participants.tsv, dataset_description.json) are always included.

To suppress companion inclusion:

ds.download(suffixes=["bold"], include_companions=False, data_dir="data/")

docs.title: Checking what will be downloaded before transferring
docs.text:
plan = ds.plan(subjects=["01"], tasks=["rest"])
print(f"{len(plan.files)} files, {plan.size_gb:.2f} GB")

docs.title: Evidence
docs.text:
Real `minimum(goal='first-batch')` plan: metadata, sidecar, events, and one BOLD run. 
plan = ds.minimum(goal='first-batch', output_dir=Path('data/ds000001'))
print(plan.to_text())

Result artifact: ds000001-minimum-first-batch.txt

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Plan — inspect the file list before downloading
  • Metadata only — fetch only JSON, TSV, bval/bvec

docs.title: Get Started
docs.text:
Qortex helps you answer a practical question before you spend time or bandwidth:

Can this neuroscience dataset support my analysis, and what is the smallest safe path to a usable artifact?

Start here if you have an OpenNeuro dataset ID, a local BIDS directory, or a model pipeline that needs source compatibility checks.

docs.title: Fastest Path
docs.text: Step Do this What you learn 1 Install Qortex Which optional extras match your modality. 2 Run the quickstart How doctor, minimum, download, conversion, and artifacts fit together. 3 Choose a tutorial How the same workflow changes for EEG, sleep, seizure, MRI, fMRI, and segmentation tasks. 4 Read the guide map Which subsystem owns the next problem you need to solve.

docs.title: Mental Model
docs.text:
Qortex is a readiness layer. It sits between raw neurodata and model code.

OpenNeuro / local BIDS
        ↓
inspect       manifest, sidecars, participants, events, headers
assess        labels, companions, sample counts, split safety
plan          exact files and bytes for one goal
download      selective, resumable transfer
visualize     QC before conversion
convert       artifact formats with provenance
use           sklearn, torch, HuggingFace, NeuroAI runtime

The important design choice is that inspection comes before transfer. Qortex uses the manifest and lightweight metadata first, then asks you to download only when a real workflow needs local bytes.

docs.title: Pick Your Entry Point
docs.text:
Dataset ID

Start with `Dataset("ds000001")`, inspect readiness, plan a first batch, convert, and open an artifact.

Local BIDS data

Run a visual audit before conversion so broken images, missing files, and geometry issues show up early.

Inference pipeline

Use NeuroAI when your question is source/model compatibility and reproducible inference output.

docs.title: Common First Questions
docs.text: Question Best page Which optional packages do I need? Install Can this dataset train a model? Can train How do I avoid downloading the full dataset? Minimum subset How do I check labels and events? Label readiness How do I inspect images before conversion? First visual audit How do I load converted data into ML code? ML bridge

docs.title: What Not To Skip
docs.text: Run doctor() before conversion. Run leakage_check() before training. Keep the artifact manifest with your model results. These three habits catch the most expensive mistakes: unusable labels, unsafe subject splits, and unreproducible training data.

docs.title: Evidence
docs.text:
Real `Dataset.manifest()` output from OpenNeuro ds000001: suffix counts and bytes by BIDS datatype. 
ds = Dataset('ds000001', snapshot='1.0.0')
manifest = ds.manifest()

Result artifact: ds000001-example-results.json

docs.title: First Visual Audit
docs.text:
A visual audit is a fast QC pass for local BIDS files. It answers:

  1. Which visualizable files are present?
  2. Which subjects or suffixes are missing?
  3. Do the thumbnails reveal obvious geometry, intensity, overlay, or loading problems?

It reads one representative slice per NIfTI file for thumbnail mode, so it is safe to run before expensive conversion.

docs.title: Real Rendered Example
docs.text: OpenNeuro `ds000001` BOLD slice rendered through `Dataset.stream_slice()`. A local visual audit uses the same visual stack across many files and writes an HTML report.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text: pip install "qortex[visual-all]"

docs.title: Run Against A Local BIDS Directory
docs.text:
from qortex import Dataset

ds = Dataset("ds000001")
report = ds.visual_audit(
    output_dir="qc/ds000001",
    local_path="data/ds000001",
    suffixes=["bold"],
    max_files=8,
)
print(report.summary())
report.to_html("qc/ds000001/visual_audit.html")

The report includes:

  • a coverage matrix
  • per-suffix counts
  • per-subject counts
  • warning summaries
  • rendered thumbnails
  • action items for missing or failed files

docs.title: CLI
docs.text:
qortex visual-audit ds000001 \
  --local data/ds000001 \
  --output-dir qc/ds000001 \
  --suffixes bold \
  --max-files 8

Add --open to launch the generated HTML report in your browser.

docs.title: Manifest-Aware Use
docs.text: When you pass local_path, Qortex compares local files to the OpenNeuro manifest. This lets the report distinguish a file that does not exist in the dataset from a file that should exist but has not been downloaded.

docs.title: Save Report Formats
docs.text:
qortex visual-audit ds000001 \
  --local data/ds000001 \
  --output-dir qc/ds000001 \
  --json \
  --markdown \
  --manifest-json

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Visual audit reference
  • fMRI QC
  • Visualize OpenNeuro
  • Download

docs.title: Evidence
docs.text:
Real BOLD axial slice streamed with `Dataset.stream_slice()` without downloading the full NIfTI file. 
sl = ds.stream_slice(subject='01', modality='bold', run='01', time_index=0, axis=2)

Result artifact: ds000001-example-results.json

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Python 3.10 or later
  • pip 23 or later

docs.title: Base install
docs.text:
The base package contains the core Dataset facade, all CLI commands, the download engine, and the readiness checks. It does not include optional modality-specific visualization or ML-framework integrations.

pip install qortex

docs.title: Optional extras
docs.text:
Qortex uses optional extras to avoid pulling in large dependencies unless you need them.

Extra What it adds Command mri nibabel, nilearn — NIfTI inspection and volume visualization pip install "qortex[mri]" dwi nibabel, dipy — DWI bval/bvec handling and gradient sphere plots pip install "qortex[dwi]" eeg mne — EEG/MEG reading, time-frequency, topomap pip install "qortex[eeg]" visual nibabel, plotly — interactive HTML viewers pip install "qortex[visual]" visual-all All of the above pip install "qortex[visual-all]" dicom pydicom — DICOM browser and metadata extraction pip install "qortex[dicom]" zarr zarr — Zarr output format pip install "qortex[zarr]" hdf5 h5py — HDF5 output format pip install "qortex[hdf5]" torch torch — PyTorch Dataset and DataLoader integration pip install "qortex[torch]" lightning lightning — LightningDataModule integration pip install "qortex[lightning]" tf tensorflow — TFRecord output format pip install "qortex[tf]" hf datasets — HuggingFace Dataset output format pip install "qortex[hf]" sklearn scikit-learn — sklearn arrays helper pip install "qortex[sklearn]" dashboard panel — Local dashboard server pip install "qortex[dashboard]" validation bids-validator — BIDS structural validation pip install "qortex[validation]" all Everything pip install "qortex[all]"

Combine extras with commas:

pip install "qortex[mri,torch,zarr]"

docs.title: Verify install
docs.text:
qortex --version
# qortex 0.x.y

Check that an optional extra loaded correctly:

from qortex.visualize import VolumeViewer  # requires [mri] or [visual]

If an extra is missing, Qortex raises an ImportError with a message that tells you exactly which extra to install.

docs.title: Development install
docs.text:
Clone the repo and install in editable mode:

git clone https://github.com/GinkgoQ/qortex.git
cd qortex
pip install -e ".[all]"

docs.title: Known install issues
docs.text:
Conflict with existing nibabel. If your environment already has nibabel installed from another package (e.g., nilearn), check that the version is compatible:

python -c "import nibabel; print(nibabel.__version__)"

Qortex requires nibabel ≥ 5.0.

Apple Silicon (MRI extras). Nibabel and nilearn install cleanly on ARM. No special flags required.

Windows. All extras work on Windows. The validation extra (bids-validator) requires Node.js on PATH if using the full JS validator. The Python-only validator shipped with newer bids-validator packages does not need Node.

docs.title: Quickstart
docs.text:
This quickstart takes one dataset ID to one local ML artifact. It uses ds000001 because it is small enough for smoke tests and has event files that expose the readiness workflow.

You will run six checks:

  1. inspect the remote manifest
  2. ask whether the dataset can support training
  3. plan the smallest useful download
  4. download only that plan
  5. convert local files to an artifact
  6. open the artifact for model code

docs.title: 1 · Inspect Before Download
docs.text:
from qortex import Dataset

ds = Dataset("ds000001")
doctor = ds.doctor()
print(doctor.to_text())

doctor() reads the manifest and lightweight metadata. It reports subjects, modalities, event coverage, companion files, size estimates, and the next action. A useful report is not always a green light; an honest uncertain state is better than pretending labels are confirmed before local files exist.

Observed result for ds000001:

Dataset : ds000001 (1.0.0)
Status  : uncertain
Summary : Event files are present, but labels need local confirmation.
Records : 80
Events  : 48/80
Labels  : 0/80
Size    : 2.42 GB
Download: True
Convert : True

docs.title: 2 · Check Training Readiness
docs.text:
training = ds.can_train(target="trial_type")
print(training.to_text())

Use this before downloading raw imaging data. Qortex checks whether label evidence exists, whether enough records can be used, and whether a subject-level split is feasible. If labels are only candidates, download metadata first and rerun with local_path.

ds.download_metadata(output_dir="data/ds000001-meta")
print(ds.can_train(local_path="data/ds000001-meta", target="trial_type").to_text())

For ds000001, can_train(target="trial_type") is intentionally cautious: the manifest shows event files, but Qortex marks labels as candidate until local event columns are inspected.

docs.title: 3 · Plan The Smallest Real Batch
docs.text:
plan = ds.minimum(goal="first-batch", output_dir="data/ds000001")
print(plan.to_text())

minimum("first-batch") returns a file list, byte estimate, reason, and primary recording. It includes companion files such as sidecars and events. This is the safest way to test a pipeline without guessing which BIDS files travel together.

`minimum("first-batch")` selected 7 files totaling about 0.05 GB: root metadata, participants, one BOLD run, its events file, and the task sidecar. The primary BOLD file dominates the bytes; the sidecars carry the interpretation.

Other useful goals:

Goal Use it when label-check You need event tables and metadata to verify labels. validation You want the smallest set that can run structural checks. first-batch You need one loadable recording plus companions.

docs.title: 4 · Download The Plan
docs.text:
ds.download_paths(plan.files, output_dir="data/ds000001")

Downloads are resumable. Re-running the same command continues incomplete files instead of starting over.

docs.title: 5 · Convert To An Artifact
docs.text:
result = ds.convert(
    output_dir="artifacts/ds000001",
    output_format="parquet",
    split_strategy="subject",
)
print(result)

Conversion writes data plus a manifest. The manifest records source files, split policy, format, provenance, and enough metadata to reopen the artifact later.

docs.title: 6 · Load For Training
docs.text:
from qortex import Artifact

artifact = Artifact.open("artifacts/ds000001")
X_train, y_train = artifact.sklearn(split="train")

For deep learning, use the framework bridge that matches your stack:

train_ds = artifact.torch(split="train")
hf_ds = artifact.huggingface(split="train")

Before training, run a split check:

print(artifact.leakage_check().to_text())

docs.title: CLI Version
docs.text:
qortex doctor ds000001
qortex can-train ds000001 --target trial_type
qortex minimum ds000001 --goal first-batch
qortex minimum ds000001 --goal first-batch --download --output-dir data/ds000001
qortex convert data/ds000001 artifacts/ds000001 --format parquet

docs.title: What You Just Learned
docs.text: Concept Why it matters Manifest-first inspection Most structural questions can be answered before raw data transfer. Evidence states possible, uncertain, and not_possible tell you how much Qortex actually knows. Goal-based planning A file plan should follow a workflow goal, not a guess about filenames. Subject-level splits Neuroimaging samples are not independent when they come from the same person. Artifact manifests Training data should be reopenable, auditable, and linked to source files.

docs.title: Reproduce The Figures
docs.text:
The figures used in this page are generated by a real Qortex run:

python scripts/generate_docs_examples.py

The script writes images to docs/assets/images/examples/ and machine-readable results to docs/assets/results/ds000001-example-results.json.

docs.title: Next Pages
docs.text:
• Readiness guide: learn each decision report.
  • Selective download: filter by subject, task, modality, suffix, and size.
  • Conversion formats: choose Parquet, HDF5, WebDataset, HuggingFace, Zarr, or TFRecord.
  • Tutorials: follow full EEG, MRI, fMRI, and segmentation workflows.

docs.title: Evidence
docs.text:
Real `minimum(goal='first-batch')` plan: metadata, sidecar, events, and one BOLD run. 
plan = ds.minimum(goal='first-batch', output_dir=Path('data/ds000001'))
print(plan.to_text())

Result artifact: ds000001-minimum-first-batch.txt

docs.title: Guides
docs.text: Use guides when you know the job and need the shortest correct route. Concept pages explain design; API pages list objects; guides tell you what to run and why that step exists.

docs.title: Pipeline At A Glance
docs.text: Stage Input Output Mistake it prevents Inspect Dataset ID or local BIDS root Manifest, entities, sidecars, participants, events, size estimates Downloading a dataset whose structure cannot support your task Assess readiness Manifest plus optional local files Doctor, can-train, label, leakage, content, and minimum-subset reports Training on missing labels, weak evidence, or unsafe splits Download A goal or file selection Resumable local subset with companions Moving terabytes when metadata or one batch would answer the question Visualize Local BIDS files or converted artifact QC HTML, overlays, thumbnails, plots, sample previews Converting unreadable files or trusting unseen geometry Convert Local BIDS subset ML artifact with splits and provenance Losing source lineage or mixing subjects across splits Use artifacts Artifact directory Arrays, datasets, split summaries, framework bridges Rebuilding loaders by hand and silently changing the training set NeuroAI Source, model, output contract Compatibility report, plan, run artifact Loading weights before the source can satisfy the model contract

docs.title: Example Evidence From A Real Dataset
docs.text:
The guide examples use public OpenNeuro dataset ds000001. The event figure below comes from Dataset.events(subject="01", task="balloonanalogrisktask", run="01") and contains 158 real events.tsv rows.

Qortex uses event tables like this to reason about label candidates, class balance, and subject coverage. For `ds000001`, labels are visible but still marked as candidate until local columns are confirmed.

docs.title: 1 · Inspect
docs.text:
Read structure first. Qortex can usually count files, subjects, modalities, events, participants, BIDS entities, and rough size before raw data is local.

from qortex import Dataset

ds = Dataset("ds000001")
print(ds.doctor().to_text())

Go to Inspect datasets.

docs.title: 2 · Assess Readiness
docs.text:
Ask whether a specific workflow is possible. Readiness reports separate confirmed facts from inferred or missing evidence.

print(ds.can_train(target="trial_type").to_text())
print(ds.minimum(goal="first-batch").to_text())

Go to Assess readiness.

docs.title: 3 · Download
docs.text:
Download a plan, not a wish. Use minimum() when you need the smallest valid subset; use selection filters when you already know the subjects, tasks, or datatypes.

plan = ds.minimum(goal="first-batch")
ds.download_paths(plan.files, output_dir="data/ds000001")

Go to Download.

docs.title: 4 · Visualize
docs.text:
Run visual QC before conversion. Fast previews catch corrupt files, strange orientations, empty masks, bad overlays, missing companions, and signal problems while the dataset is still easy to fix.

report = ds.visual_audit(local_path="data/ds000001", output_dir="qa/ds000001")
print(report.to_markdown())

Go to Visualization.

docs.title: 5 · Convert
docs.text:
Write a durable artifact. Conversion should decide windowing, labels, split policy, format, and provenance in one place.

result = ds.convert(
    output_dir="artifacts/ds000001",
    output_format="parquet",
    split_strategy="subject",
)

Go to Conversion.

docs.title: 6 · Use Artifacts
docs.text:
Open the artifact instead of re-parsing raw BIDS files in every notebook. Use framework bridges only after checking the split manifest.

from qortex import Artifact

artifact = Artifact.open("artifacts/ds000001")
print(artifact.leakage_check().to_text())
X_train, y_train = artifact.sklearn(split="train")

Go to Artifacts.

docs.title: NeuroAI Runtime
docs.text:
Use NeuroAI when your task is inference rather than dataset conversion. The runtime probes sources and model contracts before loading weights.

qortex neuroai check pipeline.yaml
qortex neuroai run pipeline.yaml --artifact-dir artifacts/run_001
qortex neuroai validate-artifact artifacts/run_001

Go to NeuroAI runtime.

docs.title: Which Guide Do I Need?
docs.text: Situation Best route I have an OpenNeuro ID and no local files Inspect → Readiness → Minimum subset I need labels for supervised learning Label readiness → Can train I want a small smoke-test download Minimum subset → Selective download I suspect local data is incomplete Content status → Local index I need to inspect images or signals Visual audit → modality-specific QC page I need Parquet, HDF5, WebDataset, or HuggingFace Conversion pipeline → Formats I need model inference and provenance NeuroAI pipeline → Outputs

docs.title: Generated Figures
docs.text: These figures are generated by python scripts/generate_docs_examples.py. They come from OpenNeuro ds000001, Qortex readiness/download reports, and Qortex NeuroAI artifact sidecars. They are not hand-drawn placeholders.

docs.title: Contact Sheet
docs.text: Single-file overview of the generated figure set.

docs.title: Dataset Structure
docs.text: `Dataset.manifest()` summarized as suffix counts and datatype byte totals. Largest files in the remote manifest, ranked before download. BOLD file coverage by subject and run. File-size spread by suffix, shown on a log scale. Suffix inventory as a table for scanning exact counts.

docs.title: Events And Labels
docs.text: Timeline and class counts from `events.tsv`. Cumulative event counts by `trial_type` over the run. Distribution of event durations. Numeric response-time distribution after non-numeric values are treated as missing.

docs.title: Image Inspection
docs.text: Remote BOLD slice streamed through `Dataset.stream_slice()`. Raw intensity distribution for the streamed BOLD slice. Mean intensity profiles across the two in-plane axes. Foreground mask used to explain visual QC thresholds. NIfTI fields used by Qortex when checking shape, TR, duration, and voxel size.

docs.title: Participants And Readiness
docs.text: `participants.tsv` age and sex fields read through `Dataset.participants()`. Age distribution grouped by the `sex` column. `CanTrainReport` chart for subject, recording, and label-ready counts. `CanTrainReport` fields as exact values.

docs.title: Download Planning
docs.text: First-batch plan with one BOLD run plus metadata and sidecars. First-batch file list with sizes. Cumulative size curve for the first-batch plan. Local availability of the first-batch files on this machine.

docs.title: Conversion And Artifacts
docs.text: Subject-safe train, validation, and test split plan.

docs.title: NeuroAI Runtime
docs.text: Segmentation showcase rendered by `render_segmentation_showcase_from_files()` from a local T1w NIfTI and a source-derived foreground candidate mask. This figure demonstrates artifact rendering, not a trained-model claim. Overlay artifact written by the same showcase run. Slice-wise mask area profile generated from the output mask volume. Contract checks before model execution. External model sources handled through Qortex adapters. Fields written to `artifact_contract.json`. Fields written to `compatibility_report.json`. Fields written to `preprocess_plan.json`. Fields written to `runtime_report.json`. Latency report broken down by runtime stage.

docs.title: Modalities
docs.text: Neurodata workflows fail in different places depending on the modality. EEG fails around channels, sampling, events, and labels. MRI fails around geometry, companions, and metadata. PET fails when units or tracer metadata are ambiguous. This section shows what Qortex checks for each data type and where to go next.

docs.title: Modality Map
docs.text: Modality BIDS datatype Primary risk Qortex support EEG eeg missing events, channel metadata, sampling mismatch, noisy channels inspect, MNE-BIDS reads, windows, feature matrices, QC, visualization, conversion MEG meg event alignment, channel metadata, acquisition-specific formats inspect, events, signal previews, conversion paths iEEG ieeg electrode coordinates, channel labels, localization metadata inspect, channel/electrode tables, coordinate readiness fNIRS nirs source/detector metadata, channel geometry, file format support inspect, MNE-backed loading, metadata checks Structural MRI anat orientation, spacing, missing masks, unreadable volumes NIfTI streaming, ortho views, masks, overlays, QC fMRI / BOLD func TR, event coverage, confounds, motion, 4D size header reads, events, fMRI QC, visual audit DWI dwi missing or mismatched bval/bvec, absent b0, shell errors companion checks, shell summary, DWI QC Fieldmaps fmap missing IntendedFor, phase metadata, distortion-correction readiness entity and sidecar inspection PET pet tracer identity, units, timing, anatomical alignment metadata checks, PET viewing, overlays Behavioral beh, events.tsv label columns, class balance, timing fields readiness, label landscape, conversion

docs.title: What Qortex Reads Before Download
docs.text: Evidence Why it matters BIDS entities Subject, session, task, acquisition, run, datatype, suffix, extension. Sidecars Sampling frequency, TR, units, task metadata, image metadata, intended-for links. Events Label candidates, trial timing, class balance, subject coverage. Participants Demographics and grouping columns for split strategy and cohort checks. File sizes Minimum-download planning and large-file warnings. NIfTI headers Shape, dtype, affine, voxel spacing, TR, orientation, endian handling.

docs.title: What Requires Local Files
docs.text: Check Needs local data because Signal QC Flat channels, spectral summaries, entropy, and autocorrelation need samples. Image QC Overlays, masks, fMRI/DWI panels, and thumbnails need image bytes. Label confirmation Candidate labels from remote events should be checked against local content. Conversion Artifacts require local source files and companions. NeuroAI inference Source/model compatibility can be planned from profiles, but prediction needs data.

docs.title: Choose A Page
docs.text:
EEG / PSG

EDF, BDF, BrainVision, EEGLAB, events, channels, windows, bandpower, PLV, CSP, and MNE-BIDS integration.

MRI family

Structural MRI, fMRI, DWI, fieldmaps, NIfTI headers, sidecars, overlays, and modality-specific QC panels.

PET

Tracer-aware metadata, units, timing, PET volumes, anatomical overlays, and conversion considerations.

MEG

MEG file structures, events, channels, signal inspection, and conversion readiness.

iEEG

Intracranial signals, electrodes, coordinate systems, and localization metadata.

Behavioral

Events tables, labels, trial types, timing, class balance, and supervised-learning readiness.

docs.title: Practical Rule
docs.text: Start with the file family that carries the model input, then inspect its companions. For example, a DWI file without matching bval and bvec is not a usable diffusion sample; an EEG file without events may still support unsupervised QC but not event-aligned classification; a PET image without tracer and unit metadata is hard to compare across subjects.

docs.title: Modality Conversion
docs.text: Qortex converts each modality to ML-ready artifacts (Parquet, Zarr, HDF5, WebDataset, HuggingFace, TFRecord). The core conversion concepts — windows, splits, labels, provenance — are the same across modalities. What differs is the per-sample shape, windowing mode, and channel selection.

docs.title: Common conversion call
docs.text:
from qortex import Dataset

ds = Dataset("ds004130", data_dir="data/ds004130/")
art = ds.convert(
    output_dir="artifacts/",
    format="parquet",
    window=dict(duration_s=2.0, overlap=0.5),
    split=dict(strategy="subject", val_frac=0.15, test_frac=0.15),
    label_col="trial_type",
)

docs.title: Per-modality output shape
docs.text: Modality Per-sample shape Notes Structural MRI (x, y, z) One sample per file fMRI BOLD (sliding) (n_voxels, n_time_points) Flattened spatial × windowed time fMRI BOLD (event-aligned) (n_voxels, n_time_points) Anchored to event onsets DWI (x, y, z, n_directions) One sample per file MEG (n_channels, n_time_points) Magnetometers + gradiometers EEG (n_channels, n_time_points) EEG-type channels only iEEG (n_channels, n_time_points) ieeg/seeg/ecog-type channels fNIRS (n_channels, n_time_points) HbO + HbR interleaved PET (x, y, z) per frame One sample per frame

docs.title: Windowing modes
docs.text:
Sliding window — fixed-length overlapping windows across the full timeseries:

window=dict(duration_s=30.0, overlap=0.5)

Best for: resting-state fMRI, MEG/EEG without discrete trials, fNIRS continuous recordings.

Event-aligned — windows centered on each trial onset in events.tsv:

window=dict(mode="event_aligned", tmin=-0.2, tmax=0.8)

Best for: task-based EEG/MEG with clear trial structure, fMRI block designs.

docs.title: Label sources
docs.text: Source Modalities How to specify events.tsv → trial_type fMRI, EEG, MEG, iEEG, fNIRS label_col="trial_type" participants.tsv → column All (subject-level) label_col="age", label_source="participants" No labels Structural MRI, DWI, unsupervised Omit label_col

docs.title: MRI-specific conversion
docs.text:
# T1w: one sample per subject, no windows
art = ds.convert(
    format="zarr",
    suffixes=["T1w"],
    split=dict(strategy="subject"),
)
# Sample shape: (x, y, z) float32

# BOLD: sliding windows, trial_type labels
art = ds.convert(
    format="parquet",
    suffixes=["bold"],
    window=dict(duration_s=20.0, overlap=0.5),
    label_col="trial_type",
)

docs.title: Signal-specific conversion (EEG / MEG / iEEG / fNIRS)
docs.text:
# Event-aligned
art = ds.convert(
    format="parquet",
    datatypes=["eeg"],
    window=dict(mode="event_aligned", tmin=-0.2, tmax=0.8),
    label_col="trial_type",
)
# Sample shape: (n_eeg_channels, n_time_points)

docs.title: DWI conversion
docs.text:
art = ds.convert(
    format="zarr",
    datatypes=["dwi"],
    split=dict(strategy="subject"),
)
# Sample shape: (x, y, z, n_directions)

bval and bvec arrays are stored as sample metadata in the artifact manifest.

docs.title: PET conversion
docs.text:
art = ds.convert(
    format="zarr",
    datatypes=["pet"],
    split=dict(strategy="subject"),
)
# One sample per PET frame if dynamic, one sample total if static

docs.title: Load the artifact
docs.text:
All modalities produce the same artifact interface regardless of shape:

from qortex import Artifact

art = Artifact.open("artifacts/")
X, y = art.sklearn(split="train")
ds_torch = art.torch(split="train")
hf_ds = art.huggingface(split="train")

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Conversion pipeline
  • Windows
  • Formats
  • ML bridge

docs.title: Modality Readiness
docs.text: Each modality has different companion files, quality indicators, and ML prerequisites. This page summarizes what Qortex checks per modality before recommending a dataset for training.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Modality Required companions Key readiness checks Structural MRI (T1w/T2w) JSON sidecar voxel size, orientation, shape fMRI BOLD JSON sidecar, events.tsv TR present, events exist, label column usable DWI bval, bvec, JSON sidecar bval/bvec present, ≥ 30 directions, multi-shell MEG channels.tsv, events.tsv channel count, SSS status, events present EEG channels.tsv, events.tsv channel count, sampling rate, events present iEEG channels.tsv, events.tsv, electrodes.tsv electrode positions present, events present fNIRS channels.tsv, events.tsv HbO/HbR channels detected, events present PET JSON sidecar (with tracer info) TracerName, FrameTimesStart, body weight

docs.title: Run readiness checks
docs.text:
The doctor() check applies modality-specific rules automatically:

from qortex import Dataset

ds = Dataset("ds004130")
report = ds.doctor()
print(report.to_text())

Use minimum() and can_train() for goal-specific decisions:

fmri_training = ds.can_train(target="trial_type", modality="fmri")
first_batch = ds.minimum(goal="first-batch", modality="fmri", target="trial_type")
print(fmri_training.to_text())
print(first_batch.to_text())

docs.title: Structural MRI
docs.text:
No events required. Labels come from participants.tsv (age, sex, group, diagnosis).

report = ds.can_train(target="group", modality="mri")
print(report.to_text())

Readiness flags: - MISSING_T1W — no T1w file for some subjects - SHAPE_MISMATCH — T1w shapes differ across subjects (preprocessing may be needed)

docs.title: fMRI BOLD
docs.text:
report = ds.can_train(target="trial_type", modality="fmri")
print(report.to_text())

Readiness flags: - NO_EVENTS — no events.tsv files - MISSING_TR — TR not in JSON sidecar - NO_COMPANIONS — events.tsv missing for some subjects that have BOLD

Real events from `ds000001/sub-01_task-balloonanalogrisktask_run-01_events.tsv`. fMRI readiness depends on this timing table as much as on the BOLD image.

docs.title: DWI
docs.text:
report = ds.doctor()
print(report.to_text())

Readiness flags: - NO_BVAL — bval file missing - NO_BVEC — bvec file missing - FEW_DIRECTIONS — fewer than 30 gradient directions (warning) - SINGLE_SHELL — only one non-zero b-value (warning for multi-shell models)

docs.title: EEG / MEG
docs.text:
training = ds.can_train(target="trial_type", modality="eeg")
print(training.to_text())

Readiness flags: - NO_CHANNELS_TSV — channels.tsv missing - NO_EVENTS — events.tsv missing or empty - LOW_SFREQ — sampling frequency below threshold (warning)

docs.title: iEEG
docs.text:
Same checks as EEG, plus:

  • NO_ELECTRODES — electrodes.tsv missing (spatial analysis not possible)
  • NO_COORDSYSTEM — coordsystem.json missing

docs.title: fNIRS
docs.text:
Same event checks as EEG, plus:

  • NO_HBO_CHANNELS — no HbO channels detected after loading
  • NO_OPTODES — optodes.tsv missing

docs.title: PET
docs.text:
• MISSING_TRACER — TracerName or TracerRadionuclide absent in sidecar
  • MISSING_FRAME_TIMES — FrameTimesStart not in sidecar (static PET assumed)
  • NO_SUV_FIELDS — BodyWeight or InjectedRadioactivity absent (SUV not possible)

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Doctor — full readiness report
  • Recipes — modality-specific recipe presets
  • Modality selection — choosing a modality

docs.title: Modality Selection
docs.text: Choosing the right modality for a machine learning project depends on the question being asked, the available hardware, and what data exists on OpenNeuro. This page helps you match a research question to a modality and then find datasets using Qortex.

docs.title: Quick guide
docs.text: Question Best modality Why Brain region activation from task fMRI BOLD Best spatial resolution for cortical mapping Fast neural dynamics (< 100 ms) EEG / MEG Millisecond temporal resolution Cortical decoding in clinical patients iEEG Direct contact with cortex; high SNR Whole-brain connectivity at rest fMRI (resting-state) Spatial coverage, established pipeline White matter tract integrity DWI Only modality directly sensitive to axon microstructure Haemodynamic response in low-cost settings fNIRS Portable, wearable; no MRI required Metabolic / receptor mapping PET Tracer-specific quantification Behavior and task performance only Behavioral / events No imaging required

docs.title: Modality characteristics
docs.text: Modality Temporal res. Spatial res. File size (typical) Online processing Structural MRI static 1 mm 30–80 MB no fMRI BOLD 0.5–2 s (TR) 2–3 mm 100 MB – 2 GB no DWI static 2–3 mm 200 MB – 2 GB no MEG < 1 ms 5–10 mm (reconstructed) 500 MB – 5 GB possible EEG < 1 ms low (scalp) 5–500 MB yes iEEG < 1 ms < 1 mm (contact) 50 MB – 5 GB yes fNIRS 0.1–1 s 1–3 cm 1–50 MB yes PET frames (15 s – 30 min) 2–5 mm 20–200 MB no

docs.title: Search by modality
docs.text:
from qortex.catalog import DatasetQuery
from qortex.client import OpenNeuroClient

# Check what's available per modality
with OpenNeuroClient() as client:
    facets = DatasetQuery().facets()
    print(facets["modalities"])
    # {"MRI": 1243, "EEG": 156, "MEG": 89, "PET": 52, ...}

# Find EEG datasets with task events, ≥ 50 subjects
results = (
    DatasetQuery()
    .modality("eeg")
    .min_subjects(50)
    .has_events()
    .fetch()
)

docs.title: Switching modality mid-project
docs.text:
Qortex's pipeline is modality-agnostic. The same Dataset → inspect → download → convert flow works for every modality. The output artifact format (Parquet, Zarr, etc.) is the same regardless of modality — only the per-sample shape changes.

# EEG: sample shape is (n_channels, n_time_points)
# fMRI: sample shape is (n_voxels, n_time_points) or (x, y, z, t_window)
# DWI: sample shape is (x, y, z, n_directions)
# PET: sample shape is (x, y, z) per frame

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Modality readiness — per-modality readiness checks
  • Modality conversion — per-modality conversion considerations
  • Search & filter — DatasetQuery reference

docs.title: Behavioral and Events
docs.text: Behavioral data in BIDS includes events.tsv files, response logs, and behavioral task records. For Qortex, behavioral data is not just supplementary — it is often what makes a dataset trainable. Events.tsv defines the trial-type labels that supervised ML depends on.

docs.title: What counts as behavioral data
docs.text:
• events.tsv — trial onsets, durations, trial types, response times (paired with every signal recording)
  • beh/ datatype — standalone behavioral tables without an imaging companion
  • participants.tsv — subject-level covariates: age, sex, diagnosis, group

docs.title: Why behavioral data matters for ML
docs.text: A BOLD or EEG file without events.tsv cannot be used for supervised classification. Qortex's readiness checks verify event coverage before you download any large imaging files.

docs.title: Behavioral pages
docs.text:
Events TSV — Structure of events.tsv files, required and optional columns, BIDS rules.

Labels and trial types — How Qortex detects label columns, evaluates class distributions, and checks supervised-learning readiness.

docs.title: Check behavioral readiness
docs.text:
from qortex import Dataset

ds = Dataset("ds000001", snapshot="1.0.0")

training = ds.can_train(target="trial_type")
print(training.to_text())

landscape = ds.label_landscape(label_column="trial_type", max_events_files=4)
print(landscape.summary())

CLI:

qortex can-train ds000001 --snapshot 1.0.0 --target trial_type
qortex eda ds004130 --label trial_type
The docs build generated this plot from a real Qortex `events()` call. The same table drives label readiness and event-aligned windows.

docs.title: Access behavioral data
docs.text:
# participants.tsv
participants = ds.participants()
print(participants.head())

# events.tsv for a subject
events = ds.events(subject="01", task="rest")
print(events.head())

# standalone behavioral file
beh_files = ds.files(datatypes=["beh"])

docs.title: Events TSV
docs.text: events.tsv is the BIDS standard for task timing and labels. It is the primary source of supervised learning labels for signal recordings (EEG, MEG, fMRI, iEEG, fNIRS).

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
onset    duration  trial_type    response_time  stim_file
0.000    1.500     fixation      n/a            n/a
1.500    1.500     go            0.412          stimuli/go_001.png
3.000    1.500     nogo          n/a            stimuli/nogo_001.png
4.500    1.500     go            0.398          stimuli/go_002.png

Required columns:

Column Description onset Event start time relative to recording start (seconds) duration Event duration in seconds (0 for instantaneous)

Recommended:

Column Description trial_type Primary label for ML; categorical condition name response_time Time from stimulus to response (seconds) — for regression tasks stim_file Path to stimulus file relative to dataset root HED Hierarchical Event Descriptors (for annotation-based workflows)

docs.title: Naming convention
docs.text:
Each events.tsv is linked to one recording via its filename entities:

sub-01/func/sub-01_task-nback_events.tsv      → sub-01_task-nback_bold.nii.gz
sub-01/eeg/sub-01_task-rest_run-01_events.tsv → sub-01_task-rest_run-01_eeg.set

docs.title: Read events.tsv
docs.text:
from qortex import Dataset

ds = Dataset("ds004130")

# From CDN (before download — events.tsv are small)
events = ds.events(subject="01", task="rest")

# After metadata download
events = ds.events(subject="01", task="rest")  # reads from local file

print(events.dtypes)        # column types
print(events.shape)         # (n_trials, n_columns)
print(events.head(5))

docs.title: Missing events.tsv
docs.text:
Qortex reports missing events.tsv in the doctor check:

report = ds.doctor()
# ERROR [NO_EVENTS]: no events.tsv found for any subject
# WARNING [PARTIAL_EVENTS]: sub-03, sub-17 missing events.tsv

Datasets with no events.tsv can still be downloaded and used for unsupervised or regression tasks where labels come from participants.tsv.

docs.title: events.json sidecar
docs.text:
An optional events.json describes columns in events.tsv:

{
  "trial_type": {
    "LongName": "Trial type",
    "Description": "Experimental condition",
    "Levels": {
      "go": "Go trial — respond to stimulus",
      "nogo": "No-go trial — withhold response"
    }
  }
}

Qortex does not currently parse events.json for column descriptions, but it is available via:

desc = ds.sidecar("sub-01/func/sub-01_task-flanker_events.json")
print(desc["trial_type"]["Levels"])

docs.title: Evidence
docs.text:
Real `events.tsv` timeline for ds000001 sub-01 run-01. 
events = ds.events(subject='01', task='balloonanalogrisktask', run='01')
print(events.shape)

Result artifact: ds000001-example-results.json

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Labels and trial types
  • Label readiness
  • Event-aligned windows

docs.title: Labels and Trial Types
docs.text: Qortex extracts labels from trial_type in events.tsv and numeric columns from both events.tsv and participants.tsv. Understanding how label detection works helps you choose the right target column and verify readiness before committing to a full download.

docs.title: Auto-detection
docs.text:
When label_column=None, label_landscape() auto-selects the best label column:

  1. Read all events.tsv files across subjects (fetched from CDN — no large download needed)
  2. Score each column by number of distinct non-null values, excluding known non-label columns: onset, duration, stim_file, HED, response_time, trial_type_id
  3. Select the column with the most distinct values that is not purely numeric
from qortex import Dataset

ds = Dataset("ds000001", snapshot="1.0.0")
landscape = ds.label_landscape(max_events_files=4)
print(landscape.summary())

Specify explicitly to avoid surprises:

training = ds.can_train(target="trial_type")
print(training.to_text())

docs.title: Label landscape
docs.text:
ds.label_landscape() shows per-class counts and missing subjects:

landscape = ds.label_landscape(label_column="trial_type", max_events_files=4)
print(landscape.summary())

Output:

Label Landscape — ds000001
Events files: 4/4 fetched
Label column: trial_type
Classes: 4  Total events: 568
Coverage: 100.0% of signal keys have events
Imbalance: 7.87x (severe)
Cross-subject consistency: 100.0%

Class distribution:
  pumps_demean                       299  ███████████████
  control_pumps_demean               187  █████████
  cash_demean                         44  ██
  explode_demean                      38  ██
Real `can_train(target="trial_type")` output for `ds000001`. The report separates label evidence from local file availability so you know what must be downloaded next.

docs.title: Regression labels
docs.text:
Numeric columns (response time, accuracy, behavioral score) can be used for regression:

# From participants.tsv
parts = ds.participants()
print(parts.select(["participant_id", "age", "diagnosis"]))

For regression-style work, inspect the candidate numeric column directly before conversion. can_train() currently reports supervised classification readiness; subject-level numeric outcomes need a conversion recipe that preserves the target and a model objective chosen outside Qortex.

docs.title: Hierarchical trial types
docs.text:
BIDS permits slash notation: condition/stimulus. Qortex treats the full string as the label by default.

landscape = ds.label_landscape(label_column="trial_type")
# Classes: ["go/compatible", "go/incompatible", "nogo"] → 3 classes

# Collapse to top level:
import polars as pl
events = ds.events(subject="01", task="flanker")
events = events.with_columns(
    pl.col("trial_type").str.split("/").list.get(0)
)
# Classes: ["go", "go", "nogo"] → 2 classes

docs.title: Class imbalance
docs.text:
Severe imbalance makes train/val/test split difficult without stratification. Check per-class counts:

landscape = ds.label_landscape(label_column="trial_type")
print(landscape.summary())

Qortex does not automatically balance classes during conversion. Handle imbalance in your training loop (class weights, oversampling, or undersampling).

docs.title: Can-train requirements
docs.text:
can_train() checks the evidence Qortex can prove from the manifest and available local files:

  • events exist and can be paired with signal files
  • the requested target appears in the behavioral tables
  • enough recordings can plausibly support a first supervised run
  • a subject-level split is feasible enough to avoid leakage

Use label_landscape() for class-balance detail, then use minimum(goal="first-batch") when the report asks for local confirmation.

docs.title: Evidence
docs.text:
Real `events.tsv` timeline for ds000001 sub-01 run-01. 
events = ds.events(subject='01', task='balloonanalogrisktask', run='01')
print(events.shape)

Result artifact: ds000001-example-results.json

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Events TSV
  • Can train
  • Label readiness
  • EDA

docs.title: EEG
docs.text: Electroencephalography (EEG) records scalp electrical potentials from electrode arrays. EEG is the most common electrophysiology modality on OpenNeuro. The EEG signal is high-dimensional in time (sampling rates 250–2048 Hz), requires companion channel and event files, and is the primary target for Qortex's event-aligned window conversion.

docs.title: Install
docs.text: pip install "qortex[eeg]"    # MNE-Python is used for all EEG reading

docs.title: BIDS structure
docs.text:
sub-01/
  eeg/
    sub-01_task-rest_eeg.set          # EEG data file
    sub-01_task-rest_eeg.fdt          # data companion (EEGLAB)
    sub-01_task-rest_eeg.json         # required sidecar
    sub-01_task-rest_channels.tsv     # channel metadata
    sub-01_task-rest_events.tsv       # trial labels
    sub-01_task-rest_electrodes.tsv   # electrode positions (optional)
    sub-01_coordsystem.json           # coordinate system (optional)

docs.title: EEG pages
docs.text:
EEG files — Formats, channel types, sampling metadata, loading.

EEG events — Events.tsv, trial types, label extraction.

EEG visualization — Butterfly plots, PSD, spectrograms, topomaps, epoched previews.

docs.title: Search for EEG datasets
docs.text:
from qortex.catalog import DatasetQuery
from qortex.client import OpenNeuroClient

# Local catalog
results = DatasetQuery().modality("eeg").min_subjects(20).has_events().fetch()

# Live — sorted by downloads
with OpenNeuroClient() as client:
    results = client.search_datasets_rich(modality="EEG", sort_by="downloads", limit=20)

CLI:

qortex search --modality eeg --min-subjects 20 --has-events

docs.title: EEG Events
docs.text: EEG events define the onset and label of each trial in a recording. Qortex reads events.tsv files and uses them to construct supervised training samples via event-aligned windowing.

docs.title: BIDS events.tsv
docs.text:
onset    duration  trial_type  response_time
0.000    1.5       rest        n/a
1.500    1.5       task        0.612
3.000    1.5       rest        n/a
4.500    1.5       task        0.498

Required columns: onset, duration. Recommended column: trial_type.

docs.title: Check event availability
docs.text:
from qortex import Dataset

ds = Dataset("ds000001", snapshot="1.0.0")
report = ds.doctor()

training = ds.can_train(target="trial_type")
print(training.to_text())

print(ds.label_landscape(label_column="trial_type", max_events_files=4).summary())
Although this figure comes from an fMRI task, the same BIDS `events.tsv` contract is used for EEG and MEG event-aligned windows: onset, duration, label, and optional response columns.

docs.title: Read events for a specific subject
docs.text:
events = ds.events(subject="01", task="balloonanalogrisktask", run="01")
print(events["trial_type"].value_counts())
print(events.shape)                    # (158, 8)

For multiple sessions or runs:

events = ds.events(subject="01", task="rest", session="01", run="01")

docs.title: Event-aligned window conversion
docs.text:
art = ds.convert(
    data_dir="data/ds004130/",
    output_dir="artifacts/",
    format="parquet",
    window=dict(mode="event_aligned", tmin=-0.2, tmax=0.8),
    label_col="trial_type",
)
# Each sample: (n_channels, n_time_points) — 200 ms pre- to 800 ms post-onset

docs.title: Stimulus channel triggers (no events.tsv)
docs.text:
Some datasets have trigger codes only in a STI or Status channel. Qortex's readiness check warns when events.tsv is absent but a stimulus channel is detected:

report = ds.doctor()
# WARNING [NO_EVENTS_TSV]: sub-03 has STI channel but no events.tsv
# Suggestion: extract events manually with mne.find_events() and save as events.tsv

docs.title: Hierarchical trial types
docs.text:
BIDS allows slash-delimited trial types: condition/stimulus. Qortex treats the full string as the label class.

# events: ["go/compatible", "go/incompatible", "nogo"]
# → 3 classes in the classifier

Collapse to top-level:

import polars as pl
events = ds.events(subject="01", task="flanker")
events = events.with_columns(
    pl.col("trial_type").str.split("/").list.get(0).alias("trial_type")
)
# → ["go", "go", "nogo"]

docs.title: Evidence
docs.text:
Real `events.tsv` timeline for ds000001 sub-01 run-01. 
events = ds.events(subject='01', task='balloonanalogrisktask', run='01')
print(events.shape)

Result artifact: ds000001-example-results.json

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• EEG files
  • Label readiness
  • Windows — event-aligned

docs.title: EEG Files
docs.text: EEG data files on OpenNeuro come in several vendor-specific formats. Qortex uses MNE-Python to read all of them through a single EEGLoader interface.

docs.title: Supported formats
docs.text: Extension Format System .set + .fdt EEGLAB MATLAB-based toolbox (most common on OpenNeuro) .edf European Data Format Clinical / portable .bdf BioSemi Data Format BioSemi ActiveTwo .fif MNE native / Elekta Converted from EEG systems .vhdr + .vmrk + .eeg BrainVision Brain Products .cnt Neuroscan Older clinical .mff EGI MFF Electric Geodesics .gdf General Data Format Open-source

docs.title: Check before download
docs.text:
from qortex import Dataset

ds = Dataset("ds004130")
eeg_files = ds.files(datatypes=["eeg"])
for f in eeg_files:
    print(f.subject, f.extension, f.size)

docs.title: Inspect after download
docs.text:
info = ds.nifti_info("sub-01/eeg/sub-01_task-rest_eeg.set")

info["sfreq"]                # 256.0  (Hz)
info["n_channels"]           # 64
info["channel_type_counts"]  # {"eeg": 61, "eog": 2, "misc": 1}
info["n_bad_channels"]       # 0
info["powerline_freq"]       # 50.0  (Hz)
info["eeg_reference"]        # "CZ"
info["software_filters"]     # {"Highpass": "0.016 Hz", "Lowpass": "100 Hz"}

docs.title: Load into Python
docs.text:
from qortex.parse.eeg import EEGLoader
from pathlib import Path

loader = EEGLoader()
record = loader.load(file_record, local_path=Path("sub-01/eeg/sub-01_task-rest_eeg.set"))

record.sfreq          # 256.0
record.n_channels     # 61  (EEG-type channels only — EOG excluded by default)
record.duration       # 480.0  (seconds)
record.channel_names  # ["Fp1", "Fp2", "F3", ...]
record.channel_types  # ["eeg", "eeg", "eeg", ...]
record.metadata["eeg_reference"]   # "CZ"
record.metadata["bad_channels"]    # []

data = loader.to_numpy(record)   # (n_eeg_channels, n_times) float64 array

Qortex selects EEG-type channels and excludes EOG, ECG, EMG, and stimulus channels by default. This is the data used for ML conversion.

docs.title: MNE-BIDS integration
docs.text: Qortex reads EEG via MNE-BIDS when a BIDS root can be inferred, which correctly applies sidecar inheritance for channel metadata. If MNE-BIDS is unavailable, it falls back to direct MNE reading.

docs.title: Lazy loading
docs.text:
record = loader.lazy_load(file_record, local_path=...)
# MNE Raw object — data stays on disk until to_numpy() or slicing

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• EEG events
  • EEG visualization
  • Channels metadata

docs.title: EEG Visualization
docs.text: Qortex visualizes EEG signals using MNE-Python for rendering and Plotly for interactive HTML output.

docs.title: Install
docs.text: pip install "qortex[eeg,visual]"

docs.title: Butterfly plot
docs.text:
Overlay all EEG channels on a single time axis. Reveals flat or noisy channels, common artifacts, and overall signal quality.

from qortex.visualize import TimeSeriesViewer

viewer = TimeSeriesViewer("data/ds004130/sub-01/eeg/sub-01_task-rest_eeg.set")
fig = viewer.butterfly()
fig.show()

CLI:

qortex visualize data/ds004130/sub-01/eeg/sub-01_task-rest_eeg.set --mode interactive

docs.title: Power spectral density
docs.text:
PSD reveals frequency-band characteristics and powerline noise:

fig = viewer.psd(fmin=1, fmax=80)
fig.show()

Expected features in clean EEG: alpha peak (8–12 Hz), 1/f slope, sharp 50/60 Hz notch if filtered.

docs.title: Spectrogram
docs.text:
Time–frequency representation:

fig = viewer.spectrogram(
    channel="Oz",
    fmin=1,
    fmax=50,
    baseline=(-0.2, 0.0),    # baseline period in seconds
)
fig.show()

docs.title: Topomap
docs.text:
Scalp distribution map at a specific time or frequency:

fig = viewer.topomap(time=0.1)           # μV at 100 ms
fig = viewer.topomap(freq_band=(8, 12))  # alpha band power
fig.show()

Topomap requires electrode positions from electrodes.tsv or a standard 10-20 layout. Qortex falls back to a standard layout if no positions are available.

docs.title: Epoched preview
docs.text:
Event-aligned epochs overlaid per trial type:

fig = viewer.epoched(
    events="data/ds004130/sub-01/eeg/sub-01_task-rest_events.tsv",
    event_id={"rest": 1, "task": 2},
    tmin=-0.2,
    tmax=0.8,
)
fig.show()

docs.title: EEG dashboard
docs.text:
